title: Opponent-response and pit-window interaction features
summary: F1 strategy is highly interactive: undercut, overcut, and safety-car windows change pit likelihood based on what nearby rivals do, not just a driver’s own tyre age.
rationale: F1 strategy is highly interactive: undercut, overcut, and safety-car windows change pit likelihood based on what nearby rivals do, not just a driver’s own tyre age. Public F1 strategy analysis and game-theoretic work both emphasize that ignoring competitor actions loses meaningful information.
implementation_hint: Build race-lap interaction features from rows already present in the tabular data: count of other cars pitting this lap and previous lap, share of field on each compound, driver tyre-life percentile within current race-lap, position bucket, and 'within undercut range' proxies such as top-10 position with old tyres and recent negative LapTime_Delta trend. Also test features conditioned on nearby position band rather than whole field.
expected_effect: Can surface strategic trigger moments that the current per-row model misses, especially clustered pit waves and defensive/undercut reactions.
risk: No explicit gap-to-car-ahead data exists, so interaction features are proxies; poorly designed aggregates may just add noise.
